summary_text:
S:
The patient reports intermittent dizziness for one week, worse when standing. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, or syncope. The patient reports a headache. The patient reports nasal congestion. The patient reports feeling light-headed. The patient reports chest pain.

O:
Blood pressure is mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. Trace pitting edema is noted to the lower extremities. The doctor observed blood pressure readings of almost 200. The patient has a systolic ejection murmur.

A:
The patient's symptoms are consistent with possible hypertension and orthostatic hypotension. A history of depression is present.

P:
The doctor wants to increase the lisinopril to 40 milligrams once a day to control the patient's high blood pressure. The doctor does not plan to start the patient on medication for depression. Start lisinopril 40 mg daily. The patient is taking lisinopril. The patient purchased a blood pressure cuff. The patient is starting therapy.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Emergency room follow-up.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Diane Baker is a 28-year-old female with a past medical history significant for depression, and hypertension, who presents for emergency room follow-up.

The patient went for a walk yesterday and began to feel lightheaded. She started to fall, however she was with her boyfriend, who caught her. She presented to the emergency room after this. While in the emergency room, her blood pressure was nearly 200 and she had a headache.

Her blood pressure is typically normal, however will become elevated approximately one week a month. She notes it is typically elevated because she is traveling a lot that week for work. While traveling, she is not eating as well and does not monitor her blood pressure. The patient is taking her lisinopril as prescribed.

Regarding her depression, she states she is doing well. Last year she started therapy and she has been going once a week, which has helped. Her support system is great and includes her boyfriend, mom, and brother.

The patient notes she has a little bit of nasal congestion, which she attributes to her seasonal allergies. She denies chest pain and shortness of breath.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Ears, Nose, Mouth and Throat: Endorses nasal congestion from seasonal allergies.
• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
• Neurological: Endorses lightheadedness and a headache.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Neck: No carotid bruits appreciable.
• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
• Musculoskeletal: Trace pitting edema in the bilateral lower extremities.

VITALS REVIEWED

• Blood Pressure: Elevated.

RESULTS

Electrocardiogram stable in comparison to last year.

Echocardiogram demonstrates decreased ejection fraction. Stable heart murmur.

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Diane Baker is a 28-year-old female with a past medical history significant for depression, and hypertension, who presents for emergency room follow-up.

PLAN

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: This is not controlled at this time. The patient presented to the emergency department yesterday with an elevated blood pressure, presyncope, and headache. She has been compliant with lisinopril, but her blood pressures have been spiking once a month based on home monitoring; however, she admits to not taking her blood pressures while traveling recently. A recent EKG revealed changes from her chronic high blood pressure.
• Medical Treatment: Increase lisinopril to 40 mg once a day.
• Patient Education and Counseling: She will continue to check her blood pressures and report them to me through the patient portal. We will have to make adjustments if necessary.

Depression.
• Medical Reasoning: The patient is doing well with weekly therapy.
• Medical Treatment: Continue current management strategies. No medication to start at this time.
• Patient Education and Counseling: She can contact me if she needs anything.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports intermittent dizziness for one week, worse when standing. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, or syncope. The patient reports a headache. The patient reports nasal congestion. The patient reports feeling light-headed. The patient reports chest pain.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Blood pressure is mildly low. Heart rate is within normal limits. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. Trace pitting edema is noted to the lower extremities. The doctor observed blood pressure readings of almost 200. The patient has a systolic ejection murmur.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's symptoms are consistent with possible hypertension and orthostatic hypotension. A history of depression is present.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: The doctor wants to increase the lisinopril to 40 milligrams once a day to control the patient's high blood pressure. The doctor does not plan to start the patient on medication for depression. Start lisinopril 40 mg daily. The patient is taking lisinopril. The patient purchased a blood pressure cuff. The patient is starting therapy.

section_miscategorizations.reason: This directly contradicts the earlier statement in the same section that he denies chest pain, creating a clinically misleading inconsistency within the subjective history. One of these should be removed or moved/clarified; as written, the placement of a second, conflicting chest pain statement is a major documentation error.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor observed blood pressure readings of almost 200.
section_miscategorizations.reason: As written, this is an observed objective finding and belongs in O, but it conflicts with the earlier statement that blood pressure is mildly low. The issue is internal inconsistency within the section rather than misplacement, so this is a minor organizational/documentation problem rather than a true section miscategorization.

This describes an existing medication the patient is already taking, which per instructions belongs in the Subjective section unless framed as a change or order. In the Plan section it can be misread as part of the new management plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient purchased a blood pressure cuff.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a past action/state reported by the patient rather than a new order or recommendation. It fits better as subjective history, though its presence in the Plan is unlikely to substantially mislead clinical management.

This reads as the patient's current treatment status (already starting therapy), which is subjective history. In the Plan section it could be misinterpreted as a new referral/order being made at this visit.
